perturbation.original_text: The term "net wholesale volume" shall mean gross sales to all customers; less returns, trade discounts and cash discounts; but no deduction shall be made for other discounts or uncollectible accounts.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "net wholesale volume" shall mean gross sales to all customers; less returns at a rate determined by Diplomat, trade discounts and cash discounts; deductions can be made for other discounts or uncollectible accounts at Diplomat's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity regarding the calculation of "net wholesale volume," particularly concerning returns and deductions for discounts and uncollectible accounts. The original definition provides a clear and objective standard, while the modified definition allows for manipulation of the royalty calculation, which is illegal.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code § 2305. Open Price Term.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2305 addresses open price terms and requires good faith in setting prices. By allowing Diplomat to unilaterally determine the return rate and discount deductions, the modified text contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing required in commercial contracts under California law. Good faith as defined in Cal. Com. Code § 1201 is 'honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing.' This standard can be violated if Diplomat unfairly inflates return rates or discount deductions.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2305. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) Nothing is said as to price; or (b) The price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) The price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as canceled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and th
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows Diplomat to unilaterally determine return rates and discount deductions, creating ambiguity and potential manipulation of "net wholesale volume." This contradicts California Commercial Code § 2305, which implies a duty of good faith in setting prices. Specifically, subsection (2) states that 'A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith.' The original text provides a clear standard, while the changed text introduces the possibility of unfair inflation of return rates or discount deductions by Diplomat, violating the good faith requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Diplomat agrees that the products covered by this Agreement shall be of such style, appearance and quality as to be adequate and suited to their exploitation to the best advantage, protection and enhancement of KI's name and the good will pertaining to such name.
perturbation.changed_text: Diplomat agrees that the products covered by this Agreement shall be of a quality no worse than what is generally acceptable by consumers.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the quality standards significantly. The original text emphasizes the 'best advantage, protection, and enhancement of KI's name,' which implies a high standard of quality. The modified text introduces a vague and lower standard of 'generally acceptable by consumers.' This shift creates uncertainty about the expected quality level and allows Diplomat to produce products of inferior quality, damaging KI's reputation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 14411 concerns the transfer of trademarks and associated goodwill. The original clause aligns with the intent of protecting the goodwill associated with Kathy Ireland's name. The modified text, by permitting a lower quality standard, potentially diminishes the goodwill and value of the trademark, effectively contradicting the protection intended by the statute. It fails to maintain a quality consistent with the existing trademark's reputation and value, possibly injuring the business reputation of the trademark holder.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 14411 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 6. BUSINESS RIGHTS [14000 - 14704] ( Division 6 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 56. ) CHAPTER 3. Trade Names and Designations [14401 - 14495] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 59. ) ARTICLE 1.5. Trade Name Registration [14411 - 14418] ( Article 1.5 added by Stats. 1972, Ch. 438. ) 14411. The filing of any fictitious business name statement by a person required to file such statement pursuant to Section 17910 shall establish a rebuttable presumption that the registrant has the exclusive right to use as a trade name the fictitious business name, as well as any confusingly similar trade name, in the county in which the statement is filed, if the registrant is the first to file such a statement containing the fictitious business name in that county, and is actually engaged in a trade or business utilizing such fictitious business name or a confusingly similar name in that county. The rebuttable presumption created by this section shall be one affecting the burden of producing evidence. (Added by Stats. 1972, Ch. 438.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the law by weakening the quality standards for products, potentially damaging the goodwill associated with the trademark, which California Business and Professions Code § 14411 intends to protect. The original text emphasizes 'best advantage, protection, and enhancement of KI's name,' indicating a high standard. The modified text uses a vague and lower standard: 'generally acceptable by consumers.' While the provided snippet of BPC 14411 speaks about rebuttable presumption of exclusive right to use a trade name, the Law Explanation explains that the lower quality standard permitted by the modification diminishes the goodwill and value of the trademark, effectively contradicting the protection intended by the statute, as the quality would no longer be consistent with the existing trademark's reputation.

perturbation.original_text: KI will participate in up to two (2) photo sessions per License Year during the period of this Agreement on behalf of Diplomat at a mutually acceptable time and place. The photo sessions shall be scheduled at KI's convenience upon not less than two (2) weeks prior notice, and shall be subject to KI's preexisting personal and professional commitments.
perturbation.changed_text: KI will participate in up to two (2) photo sessions per License Year during the period of this Agreement on behalf of Diplomat. Photo sessions shall be scheduled at Diplomat's convenience with two (2) weeks prior notice, irrespective of KI's pre-existing personal or professional commitments.
perturbation.explanation: This change fundamentally alters the control over scheduling photo sessions. The original text prioritizes KI's convenience and commitments. The modified text shifts control to Diplomat, forcing KI to participate regardless of prior obligations. This modification introduces potential conflicts and could be deemed an unreasonable imposition on KI's time and schedule.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3391(2) states that specific performance of a contract cannot be enforced against a party if it is not just and reasonable. By requiring KI to attend photo sessions regardless of prior commitments, the modified clause imposes an unreasonable burden on KI, making specific performance (i.e., forcing KI to attend the sessions) potentially unenforceable under California law. The contract becomes inherently unfair due to the shift in scheduling control.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3391 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Specific Relief [3375 - 3415] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Specific Performance of Obligations [[3384.] - 3395] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) 3391. Specific performance cannot be enforced against a party to a contract in any of the following cases: 1. If he has not received an adequate consideration for the contract; 2. If it is not, as to him, just and reasonable; 3. If his assent was obtained by the misrepresentation, concealment, circumvention, or unfair practices of any party to whom performance would become due under the contract, or by any promise of such party which has not been substantially fulfilled; or; 4. If his assent was given under the influence of mistake, misapprehension, or surprise, except that where the contract provides for compensation in case of mistake, a mistake within the scope of such provision may be compensated for, and the contract specifically enforced in other respects, if proper to be so enforced. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California Civil Code § 3391(2), as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original contract allows KI to schedule photo sessions based on their convenience. The changed text shifts this control to Diplomat and disregards KI's prior commitments. This imposes an unreasonable burden on KI, which makes the specific performance of the contract (forcing KI to attend) potentially unenforceable. Snippet 1 states that specific performance cannot be enforced if 'it is not, as to him, just and reasonable.' The change from 'KI's convenience' to 'Diplomat's convenience irrespective of KI's pre-existing personal or professional commitments' creates an unjust and unreasonable demand, thereby contradicting the law.
